title: TN IS RICH STATE |  கடன் 10 லட்சம் Vs  GDP எவ்வளவு?
body:
ஒரு மாநிலத்தின் நிதி ஆரோக்கியத்தை கடனால் அல்ல, GSDP-யில் நிதிப் பற்றாக்குறை மூலம் மதிப்பிட வேண்டும்.
அதிமுக (2021): ≈ 4.3%
திமுக (2026): ≈ 3%
FRBM வரம்பிற்குள் கொண்டு வந்து, திமுக அரசு நிதி ஒருங்கிணைப்பை உறுதி செய்துள்ளது.
தமிழ்நாட்டின் வருமானம் ரூ.100 என்றால், அதன் கடன் ரூ.26 (GSDP) மட்டுமே. ஆனால், பல மாநிலங்கள், அனுமதிக்கப்பட்ட 29% வரம்பையும் மீறிக் கடன் வாங்கியுள்ளன.
author: @IndFederal

title: TN IS RICH STATE |  கடன் 10 லட்சம் Vs  GDP எவ்வளவு?
body: எந்த கட்சியையும் சாராத என்னை போன்ற சாமானியனுக்கு புதியவர் எவர் வந்தாலும் ஏற்றுக்கொள்வதில் எந்த தயக்கமும் இல்லை.  ஆனால் இதுவரை உள்ள அவரின் சினிமா மற்றும் பொது வாழ்க்கையில் அவர் நல்லது செய்ததற்கான எந்த அறிகுறியும் இல்லை. மேலும் அவர் வாயை திறந்தாலே பொய். RBI யும் மத்திய புள்ளியியல் துறையும் ஏற்கனவே சொல்லிவிட்டது தமிழகத்தின் பொருளாதாரம் மிக சிறப்பாக உள்ளதென்று. படிக்காதவர்கள் விஜய் சொல்வதை உண்மையென்று எண்ணுவார்கள்

body: The soldier does not fight for hate of the enemy or what is in front of him,  but for love of his brothers and what's behind him.
author: @edwarddailey21

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: To all of my brothers and sisters who never made it home...... thank you for your sacrifice.........and you all live in my heart as long as it continues to beat.
author: @chibearfan292

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: My father just died last week, he was almost 90 years old.  He was in the US Marine Corps.  He fought in the Korean War and was involved in the Chosin Reservoir conflict.  I miss him already, I will miss hearing his stories of his days in the Corps.  It is like muscle memory, I keep thinking I can see him again, go to his house, call him on the phone. But no, he is not there.  I miss him so much......I am going to move in to his house and live there.  I have his Purple Heart and other things from his time in the Marine Corps,  Rest In Peace Dad, you have earned it......love you.......
author: @lindaturner6033

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: “I am a grumpy veteran.  I served, I sacrificed, I don’t regret.  I am not a hero, not a legend.  My oath of enlistment has no expiration date.  I have anger issues and a serious dislike for stupid people.  I am proud to be a veteran!”  So we’re my parents, and their parents, back to the founding of our country.  “I am proud to be a veteran!  If this offends you, I don’t care!”
author: @ExiledPiasa

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: This song was sung and composed by the Grandson of a Scottish  soldier who died in WW1 staying with his wounded compatriots and died defending them .

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body:
This song means a lot to me. I am a 100% permantly disabled Veitnam vet.
I have been like this since June 1972 and have never been ashamed one second or less.
I will take my Purple Heart, my jump wings, and my pride to my grave.

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: I am the son of a Viet Nam vet who made it home. I came home from Desert Storm. My son has returned from Iraq and Afghanistan (3 tours) so far. Currently traveling 2 days after Memorial Day to visit him at Ft Benning where he is a Drill Sargent. This song always touches my heart.

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: I’m a Vietnam veteran, and I belong to a squad of other veterans who preform a Soldier’s cross ceremony at veteran funerals. I am very proud & honored to be part of it. Sometimes we do the ceremony in conjunction with some active duty personnel, one funeral had a bagpiper playing this song & a Master Sergeant singing it, as a person of Scottish blood, I’m not ashamed to say I shed a tear. There’s something about the song that is very powerful & pulls at you.
author: @Sunman2050

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body:
35 years ago, 37 men died on the USS Stark. I listened over the satcom radio as the entire situation played out. From the warning to identify to the call for help. The sound of the voice of the young Officer of the Deck as he called for help will never be forgotten.

Here’s to those brave men who died on May 17, 1987.

May they be granted Eternal Rest.
author: @vicgarcia1515

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: All gave some, some gave all. Thank you United States veterans. I have a pretty easy life in our beautiful country because of you
author: @JoshuaDixon-wc7xd

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: I served in the United States Air Force for 25 years. I took the same oath 6 times over the years.  The first time I recited the oath, it was the Oath, All the other times I recited it, it was just a reaffirmation of the original oath I took......No one has ever relieved me of my oath and no one can or will.........."I, _____, do solemnly swear (or affirm) that I will support and defend the Constitution of the United States against all enemies, foreign and domestic; that I will bear true faith and allegiance to the same; and that I will obey the orders of the President of the United States and the orders of the officers appointed over me, according to regulations and the Uniform Code of Military Justice. So help me God."
author: @DanielRandles-y6c

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: My father was a vietnam vet. He died from the chemicals our government used. Ĥe will always be MY hero.

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body: We laid to rest my wife's grandfather today.  He was 98 years old and was a WWII Navy Veteran.  Fair Winds and Following Seas.
author: @erikcox1947

title: Lay Me Down in the Cold, Cold Ground
body:
For my Father;
Wilson, Kenneth C.
SN; 1064067 USMCR
Chosin, 1950
No Boot Camp for Reserves, only
his monthly drills, and 2 2 week summer camps.
Wounded 3 times, plus severe frostbite of his. He was awarded the Bronze Star w Bronze V, for Valor. He was one of the first Americans, to face the Chinese Communist in battle. They were always outnumbered 5 and sometimes 10 to 1.
Every member of his fire team got hit, but no one died. He told me "that was the miracle."
He died, in his sleep at age 84.
author: @truthbtold2910

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: Its bs when a man gets a life sentence for drugs but a child predator gets under 5 in some cases probation:
author: @Bean88130

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I love this guy because he never tries to put a narrative spin on these videos. He just lets the interviewees speak for themselves honestly without judgement and encourages positivity. The people know they’ve done bad things but are still treated like human beings while not framing them as victims or heros, just real people with real lives. He’s like a much better version of Tyler Olivera, someone who try’s to push narratives that were predetermined before the filming. It’s good to see someone let people give honest accounts of their own experiences, no matter how varied they may be.
author: @oscarlindahl5662

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I met one of my best friends by going to jail for six months. Shared a room with him for months while we awaited sentencing. I ended up getting moved to a program and on the way out, he gave me a couple bags of coffee and a rack of Ramen to take since I couldn't buy commissary for two weeks. Now we have both been out for over six years and stay in contact, go to baseball games and just check in on each other. I'm grateful for that aspect of jail.
author: @SeanSinha

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I did 8 on a 12 for Agg assault w/deadly weapon on a public servant in TDCJ (Estelle, Wynne, Dalhart, Hughes, Ellis, Byrd, Holliday, Montford). I was released in 2016 from a halfway house in Houston, with nothing but some clothes and a Pitbull they'd given me.  Today, eight years later, I have earned Two master's degrees; a Master's in IT and a Master's in CS/Cyber Security Engineering which I just completed. I received my Bachelor's in Cyber Security four years after my release, while working full-time. Now, after prison, I have the life I should have always lived, clean, sober, and FREE.  "CHOICES."
author: @unsilent369

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I was in prison for a bit and the amount of guys on their 3rd, 4th, even 6th bid blew my mind. I don’t think I ever talked to a guy that was actually innocent the whole time. I was guilty. Was a 22 month sentence I got parole in 366 days served by taking every class, program, and job available. That was 2018 when I got out and I’m not on probation or anything now. Been sober since 2021. Realized I had never been arrested when I was 100% sober. It’s a choice, 99% of the time it is a choice to break the law and you get what you get. Lost count how many people I met that I was glad they were locked up. There are evil people in this world, believe that. Truly evil, no chance of changing, wanna just burn it all down type people. Be glad there is a prison system for that reason.
author: @portlandGTR

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: "my aim is to find the humanity among these men"this is why I support your channel, its more than just hype and shock value
author: @campar1043

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas

body: It's great to see the changes that these brothers are making with their lives. I did 25 years for bank robbery in the Georgia Department of Corrections. I have been out since 2005. My first 15 years on the outside. I went back into prison to help other guys to change their life. I've also written five books since I have been out. One of those books is called. " Breaking the Cycle of Recidivism: Gettin' Out and Goin' Straight." Keep up the great work..

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body:
Back in the 80's when I was in the military, I was wrongfully court-martialed for something I wasn't responsible for. I was given 8 months of confinement. (Yeah, I know. It wasn't much.) When I got to the facility, I was lead through a common area, past barred areas that held a bunch of bunkbeds, all the way to the back, down a small hall. I was placed in a metal box, with bars for a door, a cot, school type desk and that was it. I was allowed reading material, and that was it. Each day, the sergeant that ran the place would come back and see me, and ask how I was doing. I would stand at attention and say "I'm doing fine". After the third day, he asked me again and again, I'd say I was fine. He then said, you know, I don't get you. I asked, why's that sergeant? He said, each day you keep saying you're fine. You never ask to be let out. What's wrong with you? I said, I didn't know I could get out. I thought this was going to be my cell for the next 8 months. So, why fight something I can't change. I've got food, a place to sleep, reading material. That's all I need, so I'm fine. I'm not like depressed, or upset or anything.
He said, and here I thought maybe you were crazy or something. You're not supposed to be fine in here all by yourself. Then he laughed and opened the door. He said, I usually let people out the first day, but you kept saying you were fine.
I had no problem being locked up all by myself. I've always been kind of a loaner, in a way. If something ever happened and I got locked up, I'd rather be in solitary all by myself, than to be around a bunch of hardened criminals that may be looking for a new guy to attack for no reason. Then, one of two things are going to happen. Either I'll kill someone, or they'll kill me. And, I don't like either of those two outcomes.
Oh, and by the way, I appealed my court-martial and won three years later. I got 3 years of backpay including raises, got my rank back, and since I had one year left on my last enlistment, I went back in. Unfortunately, due to the Clinton cut back on the military, I was among many others on base that was told, your services are no longer required. Pissed me off. Spend 3 years fighting to clear my name, get it all back just to have it yanked from me again. At least they paid me to get out.
author: @macD723

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas

title: 72 Hours Inside State Trooper Academy | Ep.01

My neighbor went through this in 2017 and he was in the Marine Corps before he went to the Pennsylvania State Police academy. The Academy he said was harder than Marine Boot Camp. I went to his graduation which is really cool and toured the barracks.

Also a few years ago the academy was doing tear gas training and some of it entered nearby Hersheypark which caused some guests over there to be impacted by the gas. A section of the park was evacuated.

body: Seeing this program, I am very grateful for my freedom. It isn’t worth breaking the law because if you end up in prison, you forego all your rights. Simple rule is, if you want your freedom and value it, don’t break the law
author: @biancasjourney9822

title: Inside Death Row with Trevor McDonald | DOUBLE EP Feature | True Crime Documentary
body: I come from a family of Law Enforcement. Los Angeles County Sheriffs. I have seen the impact on both sides. I personally have been incarcerated multiple times. It is such a complex system. And no story or situation is the same. But after 20 years in the medical field, I have gone back to get an education so that I can dedicate my time to fixing the prison re-entry and restorative system. As a community and a society, we need  to do better. For not just inmates but for the victims. I absolutely love watching these documentaries. It keeps me motivated to do what Im doing.
author: @shannonanderson2674

title: Tennessee State University Campus Tour (Part III)
body: A new book/ebook: "What Foreigners Need To Know About America From A To Z: How to understand crazy American culture, people, government, business, language and more.” Endorsed worldwide by ambassadors, educators, and editors, it paints a revealing picture of complex America to help those who will benefit from a better understanding, including international students.  It also has chapters that identify the most common English grammar and speech problems foreigners have. Good luck to all!
